children.name: Callaeas
children.description: The kokakos — a genus of large, arboreal passerines endemic to New Zealand, with grey plumage and striking blue or blue-black wattles on the face. They are largely frugivorous, with a specialised gizzard that helps process tough forest fruits. Their haunting, organ-like duetting song carries through the forest and is considered among the most beautiful of any New Zealand bird.

children.name: Callaeas cinereus
children.description: A large, pale-grey forest bird with a short, slightly downcurved bill and vivid blue wattles on the face, endemic to New Zealand. It is a strong flier despite its relatively short wings, using its long, broad tail for steering through dense forest. Its glorious, flute-like duetted song, often described as the finest bird music in New Zealand, resonates through the remaining native forests where intensive predator control has allowed populations to recover.

children.name: Philesturnus
children.description: The saddlebacks — a genus of striking black-and-chestnut birds endemic to New Zealand, named for the saddle-like chestnut patch across the back. They are highly active insectivores that forage on tree trunks and branches, probing bark crevices with their stout bills. They have been extensively translocated to predator-free islands and sanctuaries as a conservation measure.

children.name: Philesturnus rufusater
children.description: A striking black bird with a rich chestnut saddle and prominent orange-red wattles on either side of the bill, endemic to the North Island of New Zealand. It was once restricted to a single small island but has been successfully translocated to multiple predator-free sanctuaries. It is an inquisitive, acrobatic forager that probes bark and dead wood for insects, often hanging upside-down in its search.

children.name: Philesturnus carunculatus
children.description: A black bird with a chestnut saddle and larger, brighter orange-red wattles than its North Island relative, endemic to the South Island of New Zealand. Like the North Island Saddleback, it is an arboreal insectivore that gleans prey from bark and epiphytes in mature native forest. It has been, and remains, the focus of intensive conservation management involving island translocations and predator eradication.
